title: Should You Stay or Should You Go?
summary: Priya, who has lived in the same Australian city for years, and Liam, who is considering a move, debate the pros and cons of relocating. Their conversation touches on career, friendships, and what it means to feel at home.

body.speaker: Priya
body.text: So you're really thinking about moving to Melbourne? Like, actually leaving?

body.speaker: Liam
body.text: I've been thinking about it seriously for about six months now. The job market there is just so much stronger for what I do. And honestly, I feel like I've outgrown this city a little bit.

body.speaker: Priya
body.text: I hear you, but don't you think you'd miss everything here? Your whole network is in Brisbane. Your family, your friends, all the places you know. Isn't that worth something?

body.speaker: Liam
body.text: Of course it's worth something. But I don't want to look back in ten years and feel like I stayed because I was scared to change. A lot of people do that.

body.speaker: Priya
body.text: That's fair, but I think there's a difference between staying out of fear and staying because you've built something real. I have a life here. Deep roots. A community I actually care about.

body.speaker: Liam
body.text: I'm not saying what you have isn't valuable. I'm just saying it's not where I am right now. And I do think you can rebuild those things somewhere new. It's harder, but it's possible.

body.speaker: Priya
body.text: Can you though? I've seen friends move away chasing a career and then come back five years later saying they never really felt at home there. You can't just recreate friendships from scratch.

body.speaker: Liam
body.text: Maybe. But you can make new ones. And some of my closest relationships now are with people I met after I left my hometown at twenty-two. I wasn't born with my friends — I found them. I can find more.

body.speaker: Priya
body.text: Okay, but what about identity? I feel like this city is part of who I am. The places I grew up, the people who knew me when I was young. That kind of continuity matters.

body.speaker: Liam
body.text: I actually think moving forces you to figure out who you really are, separate from the context you grew up in. When everything familiar is stripped away, what's left is the real you. I find that kind of clarity appealing.

body.speaker: Priya
body.text: Or destabilising. I genuinely think that not everyone needs to uproot themselves to grow. Some of the most interesting people I know have spent their whole lives in one place and developed incredible depth.

body.speaker: Liam
body.text: You're right, I'm not saying everyone should move. I'm just saying it's the right thing for me at this point in my life. I want new challenges. I want to be somewhere that pushes me.

body.speaker: Priya
body.text: What if Melbourne doesn't work out? Have you thought about a backup plan?

body.speaker: Liam
body.text: I have. If it doesn't work out after two years, I'll reassess. But at least I'll know I tried. Not knowing is worse.

body.speaker: Priya
body.text: I suppose I respect that. I just couldn't do it. Brisbane feels like home to me in a way I don't think I'd ever find somewhere else.

body.speaker: Liam
body.text: And that's completely valid. You've found your place. I just haven't found mine yet.

annotations.meaningVi: đã phát triển vượt quá, không còn phù hợp nữa
annotations.exampleEn: She felt she had outgrown her old job and needed a new challenge.

annotations.meaningVi: mạng lưới (quan hệ xã hội/nghề nghiệp)
annotations.exampleEn: Building a professional network takes time and effort.

annotations.meaningVi: gốc rễ sâu bền, mối liên hệ lâu dài với một nơi
annotations.exampleEn: Her family has deep roots in the local community.
He moved cities several times while chasing a career in finance.

annotations.meaningVi: sự liên tục, không gián đoạn
annotations.exampleEn: The continuity of the relationship was important to her.

annotations.meaningVi: bị lấy đi, bị loại bỏ
annotations.exampleEn: When his wealth was stripped away, he found out who his real friends were.
A period of quiet helped her gain clarity about her future.

annotations.meaningVi: gây mất ổn định, gây bất an
annotations.exampleEn: The sudden change of leadership was destabilising for the team.

annotations.meaningVi: tự nhổ bật rễ, rời bỏ nơi quen thuộc
annotations.exampleEn: Many families uprooted themselves to find work in the cities.
It's wise to have a backup plan in case things don't work out.
After a year, they reassessed their strategy.

annotations.meaningVi: ở lại vì sợ hãi chứ không phải vì lựa chọn
annotations.exampleEn: Staying out of fear rarely leads to happiness.

annotations.meaningVi: thúc đẩy tôi, khiến tôi phát triển
annotations.exampleEn: I need an environment that pushes me to do my best.

annotations.meaningVi: tìm được chỗ đứng của mình
annotations.exampleEn: After years of searching, she finally found her place in life.

annotations.meaningVi: hoàn toàn hợp lý, đáng được tôn trọng

grammarNotes.title: Second Conditional — Expressing hypothetical future scenarios
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Cả hai nhân vật dùng điều kiện loại 2 để nói về những điều có thể xảy ra trong tương lai giả định. 'If it doesn't work out after two years, I'll reassess' (thực ra đây là điều kiện loại 1 — khả năng có thể xảy ra). Để phân biệt: điều kiện loại 1 (if + present, will) dùng cho khả năng thực tế; điều kiện loại 2 (if + past, would) dùng cho tình huống ít khả năng hơn hoặc giả thuyết. Trong hội thoại này, Priya ngầm dùng cấu trúc giả định khi hỏi 'don't you think you'd miss everything here?'
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Both speakers use conditional structures to discuss future scenarios. 'If it doesn't work out after two years, I'll reassess' is a first conditional (realistic possibility: if + present, will). Contrast with second conditional (if + past, would) for hypothetical/unlikely situations. Priya implicitly uses the conditional in 'don't you think you'd miss everything here?' — the 'd (would) signals a hypothetical.

grammarNotes.title: Discourse Markers — Conceding a point and countering
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Hội thoại sử dụng nhiều liên từ và cụm từ để thừa nhận một quan điểm trước khi phản bác: 'That's fair, but...', 'Of course it's worth something. But...', 'You're right, I'm not saying...', 'I suppose I respect that.' Những cụm này giúp cuộc tranh luận nghe lịch sự và tự nhiên hơn, không gay gắt. Đây là kỹ năng quan trọng trong tiếng Anh giao tiếp học thuật và chuyên nghiệp.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The dialogue uses discourse markers to concede before countering: 'That's fair, but…', 'Of course it's worth something. But…', 'You're right, I'm not saying…'. These phrases make disagreement sound respectful and natural. Mastering them is key for academic and professional communication in English.

translationVi:
Priya: Vậy bạn thực sự đang nghĩ đến việc chuyển đến Melbourne? Như là, thực sự rời đi?
Liam: Tôi đã suy nghĩ nghiêm túc về điều này khoảng sáu tháng rồi. Thị trường việc làm ở đó đang mạnh hơn rất nhiều với lĩnh vực của tôi. Và thành thật mà nói, tôi cảm thấy mình đã phát triển vượt qua thành phố này một chút rồi.
Priya: Tôi hiểu bạn, nhưng bạn không nghĩ mình sẽ nhớ mọi thứ ở đây sao? Toàn bộ mạng lưới của bạn ở Brisbane. Gia đình, bạn bè, tất cả những nơi quen thuộc. Chẳng đáng sao?
Liam: Tất nhiên là đáng. Nhưng tôi không muốn nhìn lại mười năm sau mà cảm thấy mình đã ở lại vì sợ thay đổi. Nhiều người làm vậy lắm.
Priya: Điều đó công bằng, nhưng tôi nghĩ có sự khác biệt giữa ở lại vì sợ hãi và ở lại vì bạn đã xây dựng được điều gì đó thực sự. Tôi có cuộc sống ở đây. Gốc rễ sâu bền. Một cộng đồng tôi thực sự quan tâm.
Liam: Tôi không nói những gì bạn có là không có giá trị. Tôi chỉ nói đó không phải là nơi tôi đang đứng bây giờ. Và tôi nghĩ bạn có thể tái tạo những điều đó ở một nơi mới. Khó hơn, nhưng có thể.
Priya: Thật sự vậy không? Tôi đã thấy bạn bè chuyển đi theo đuổi sự nghiệp rồi về lại sau năm năm nói họ không bao giờ thực sự cảm thấy ở nhà ở đó. Bạn không thể tái tạo tình bạn từ đầu như vậy được.
Liam: Có thể vậy. Nhưng bạn có thể kết bạn mới. Và một số mối quan hệ thân thiết nhất của tôi hiện nay là với những người tôi gặp sau khi rời quê nhà năm hai mươi hai tuổi. Tôi không sinh ra cùng bạn bè — tôi tìm thấy họ. Tôi có thể tìm thêm.
Priya: Được rồi, nhưng còn bản sắc thì sao? Tôi cảm thấy thành phố này là một phần của con người tôi. Những nơi tôi lớn lên, những người đã biết tôi khi còn trẻ. Sự liên tục đó quan trọng lắm.
Liam: Tôi thực ra nghĩ rằng việc chuyển đi buộc bạn phải hiểu mình thực sự là ai, tách biệt khỏi bối cảnh bạn lớn lên. Khi tất cả những điều quen thuộc bị lấy đi, thứ còn lại chính là con người thật của bạn. Tôi thấy sự rõ ràng đó hấp dẫn.
Priya: Hoặc gây mất ổn định. Tôi thực sự nghĩ không phải ai cũng cần nhổ bật rễ để phát triển. Một số người thú vị nhất tôi biết đã dành cả cuộc đời ở một nơi và phát triển chiều sâu đáng kinh ngạc.
Liam: Bạn nói đúng, tôi không nói ai cũng nên chuyển đi. Tôi chỉ nói đó là điều đúng đắn với tôi ở giai đoạn này trong cuộc đời. Tôi muốn những thách thức mới. Tôi muốn ở nơi thúc đẩy mình.
Priya: Nếu Melbourne không thành công thì sao? Bạn đã nghĩ đến kế hoạch dự phòng chưa?
Liam: Rồi. Nếu không thành sau hai năm, tôi sẽ đánh giá lại. Nhưng ít nhất tôi sẽ biết mình đã thử. Không biết còn tệ hơn.
Priya: Tôi đoán tôi tôn trọng điều đó. Tôi chỉ không thể làm vậy. Brisbane cảm giác như nhà với tôi theo cách mà tôi không nghĩ mình sẽ tìm thấy ở nơi nào khác.
Liam: Và điều đó hoàn toàn hợp lý. Bạn đã tìm được chỗ của mình. Tôi chỉ chưa tìm được chỗ của mình thôi.

questions.prompt: How long has Liam been seriously thinking about moving to Melbourne?
options: Since he was offered a job there
questions.explanation: Liam says: 'I've been thinking about it seriously for about six months now.'
questions.hint: Liam gives a specific time frame early in the conversation.

questions.prompt: What is Liam's main professional reason for wanting to move?
options: He has already accepted a job offer in Melbourne
The job market in Melbourne is much stronger for his field
He wants to study at a university in Melbourne
questions.explanation: Liam says 'The job market there is just so much stronger for what I do.'
questions.hint: He gives a career-related reason in his very first response.

questions.prompt: What does Priya mean when she says she has 'deep roots' in Brisbane?
options: She has lasting connections — family, friends, and community — in the city
She grew up in a rural area near Brisbane
She works in the city's environmental sector
questions.explanation: 'Deep roots' metaphorically describes lasting connections to a place — Priya has family, friends, and community ties.
questions.hint: Think about what 'roots' means figuratively when applied to a person and a place.

questions.prompt: What concern does Priya raise about people who move cities for their career?
options: They often earn less money than expected
They struggle to advance professionally in a new city
Many come back years later saying they never felt truly at home
They lose touch with professional contacts in their home city
questions.explanation: Priya says she has 'seen friends move away chasing a career and then come back five years later saying they never really felt at home there.'
questions.hint: Priya uses personal examples of people she knows.

questions.prompt: What point does Liam make about his own existing friendships?
options: His best friends all live in Melbourne already
He has kept in touch with all his childhood friends
Some of his closest relationships are with people he met after leaving his hometown
He has few close friends in Brisbane because he has always been introverted
questions.explanation: Liam says 'some of my closest relationships now are with people I met after I left my hometown at twenty-two.'
questions.hint: Liam uses this to argue that meaningful friendships can be formed later in life.

questions.prompt: What does Liam believe happens to a person when they move away from familiar surroundings?
options: They become more dependent on their family back home
They are forced to figure out who they really are
They gradually lose their original cultural identity
They become more professional and career-focused
questions.explanation: Liam says moving 'forces you to figure out who you really are, separate from the context you grew up in.'
questions.hint: Liam frames this as a positive outcome.

questions.prompt: How does Priya counter Liam's point about self-discovery through relocation?
options: She says travelling is a better alternative to actually moving
She says the process can be destabilising, and people can grow deeply without uprooting themselves
She says most people who move abroad end up feeling more lost
She says identity is mainly shaped by career, not location
questions.explanation: Priya says it can be 'destabilising' and that 'not everyone needs to uproot themselves to grow', noting some people develop incredible depth in one place.
questions.hint: Priya offers an alternative vision of personal growth.

questions.prompt: What is Liam's backup plan if Melbourne does not work out?
options: He will move to another city instead
He will return to Brisbane and resume his previous career
He will reassess after two years
He has not thought about a backup plan yet
questions.explanation: Liam says: 'If it doesn't work out after two years, I'll reassess.'
questions.hint: Liam mentions a specific time frame for his reassessment.

questions.prompt: At the end of the conversation, does Priya change her own position about staying in Brisbane?
options: Yes, she admits she is also considering a move
Yes, she agrees that Liam's approach is best for everyone
No, she maintains that Brisbane feels like home and she could not find that elsewhere
No, she ends the conversation without giving her own view
questions.explanation: Priya says: 'Brisbane feels like home to me in a way I don't think I'd ever find somewhere else.'
questions.hint: Priya ends by contrasting her own position with Liam's.

questions.prompt: How does Liam describe himself and Priya at the very end of the dialogue?
options: He says Priya is right and he will reconsider his decision
He says Priya has found her place, while he has not yet found his
He says they are in completely different stages of life and cannot understand each other
He says they are both searching for the same thing in different ways
questions.explanation: Liam says: 'You've found your place. I just haven't found mine yet.'
questions.hint: His final line is a short, clear statement of difference.

cloze.template:
Priya: So you're really thinking about moving to Melbourne? Like, actually leaving?
Liam: I've been thinking about it seriously for about six months now. The job market there is just so much stronger for what I do. And honestly, I feel like I've {{b1}} this city a little bit.
Priya: I hear you, but don't you think you'd miss everything here? Your whole {{b2}} is in Brisbane. Your family, your friends, all the places you know. Isn't that worth something?
Liam: Of course it's worth something. But I don't want to look back in ten years and feel like I stayed because I was scared to change. A lot of people do that.
Priya: That's fair, but I think there's a difference between staying out of fear and staying because you've built something real. I have a life here. Deep {{b3}}. A community I actually care about.
Liam: I'm not saying what you have isn't valuable. I'm just saying it's not where I am right now. And I do think you can rebuild those things somewhere new. It's harder, but it's possible.
Priya: Can you though? I've seen friends move away chasing a career and then come back five years later saying they never really felt at home there. You can't just recreate friendships from {{b4}}.
Liam: Maybe. But you can make new ones. And some of my closest relationships now are with people I met after I left my hometown at twenty-two. I wasn't born with my friends — I found them. I can find more.
Priya: Okay, but what about identity? I feel like this city is part of who I am. The places I grew up, the people who knew me when I was young. That kind of {{b5}} matters.
Liam: I actually think moving forces you to figure out who you really are, separate from the context you grew up in. When everything familiar is {{b6}}, what's left is the real you. I find that kind of clarity appealing.
Priya: Or {{b7}}. I genuinely think that not everyone needs to uproot themselves to grow. Some of the most interesting people I know have spent their whole lives in one place and developed incredible depth.
Liam: You're right, I'm not saying everyone should move. I'm just saying it's the right thing for me at this point in my life. I want new challenges. I want to be somewhere that {{b8}} me.
Priya: What if Melbourne doesn't work out? Have you thought about a {{b9}} plan?
Liam: I have. If it doesn't work out after two years, I'll {{b10}}. But at least I'll know I tried. Not knowing is worse.
Priya: I suppose I respect that. I just couldn't do it. Brisbane feels like home to me in a way I don't think I'd ever find somewhere else.
Liam: And that's completely valid. You've found your place. I just haven't found mine yet.

blanks.explanation: 'Outgrown' means to have developed beyond something — to reach a stage where something no longer suits you.
'Network' in a professional/social context means your extended web of relationships and contacts.
'Deep roots' is a figurative expression for strong, lasting connections to a place — like tree roots that go deep into the ground.
'From scratch' is a fixed idiomatic phrase meaning from the very beginning with nothing already in place.
'Continuity' means an unbroken chain or consistency over time — here Priya means the ongoing thread of knowing the same people and places.
'Stripped away' is the specific phrasal verb used — it suggests a more complete and deliberate removal.
'Destabilising' means causing a loss of stability or security — Priya suggests that Liam's 'clarity' could feel unsettling rather than liberating.
'Pushes me' in this context means challenges or forces you to develop — it is a common colloquial expression in English.
'Backup plan' is the idiomatic term for a secondary plan in case the main one fails.
'Reassess' means to evaluate or judge again — it implies a formal, considered review of the situation.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Priya and Liam represent two very different ideas about what it means to grow as a person — one through staying and building depth in one place, the other through leaving and discovering who you are without familiar context. Which approach do you think leads to a richer sense of self, and does the answer depend on the person or the situation?
